serving.natural_language_portion: One raw quail egg weighs approximately 9 g and is commonly consumed in multiples of 3–6 eggs per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Quail egg, whole, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient and primary micronutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for quail egg, whole, raw (cr_id F01060014, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and additional micronutrients not reported by HPB were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01140 (Egg, quail, whole, fresh, raw), which is an exact food match and an approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Quail egg, whole, raw contains only 0.41 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates and cannot elicit a meaningful glycaemic response. The value 0 is used here as a numeric sentinel indicating 'not applicable' rather than a true measured GI of zero; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to make this explicit. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. No GI entry exists or is expected in the University of Sydney GI Database for whole raw eggs.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (141 mg/100 g) with moderate potassium (132 mg/100 g); no added salt or processed ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Quail eggs are acceptable within a hypertension-management diet when prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces; however, the very high cholesterol content (844 mg/100 g) warrants limiting intake to 3–4 eggs per occasion for individuals with concurrent cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.41 g/100 g) and sugar (0.4 g/100 g); high protein (13.05 g/100 g) supports glycaemic neutrality
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw quail eggs have a negligible glycaemic impact and are suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; protein content slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-meal glucose excursions, but preparation without added sugar or sweet sauces is essential, and lipid monitoring is advised given the high cholesterol content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Exceptionally high dietary cholesterol (844 mg/100 g) — approximately 2.8× the 300 mg/day reference limit in a single 30 g serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit quail egg consumption to occasional small servings and discuss frequency with their physician, as the cholesterol load is substantial even in a single serving; saturated fat (3.56 g/100 g) is moderate and below the 5 g/100 g threshold, but the cumulative cholesterol burden from multiple eggs is clinically significant.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High cholesterol (844 mg/100 g) elevates cardiovascular risk in RA patients on corticosteroids; no significant anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Quail eggs provide useful calcium (64 mg/100 g) and iron (3.65 mg/100 g) for RA patients on corticosteroids, but the very high cholesterol content requires portion restriction; deep-frying should be avoided as advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from high-heat cooking worsen joint inflammation, and raw consumption carries Salmonella risk — boiling or poaching is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Quail egg, whole, raw refers to the uncooked, intact egg of the common quail (Coturnix coturnix japonica). The egg is distinctively small — approximately 9 g each — with a cream-coloured shell heavily speckled with brown and black spots. The interior contains a proportionally large, deep-yellow yolk relative to the white, giving the egg a richer flavour and denser nutritional profile than a standard chicken egg. Raw quail eggs are consumed across East Asia, Southeast Asia, and Europe, commonly hard-boiled as street food, skewered in satay, added to hot pot, or served alongside noodle dishes. In Singapore and Malaysia, they are frequently found in hawker dishes such as satay bee hoon, laksa, and char kway teow. Nutritionally, quail eggs are high in protein (13.05 g/100 g), moderate in fat (11.09 g/100 g), and extremely low in carbohydrates (0.41 g/100 g), making them glycaemically neutral. They are notably rich in cholesterol (844 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.58 mcg/100 g), riboflavin (0.79 mg/100 g), iron (3.65 mg/100 g), phosphorus (226 mg/100 g), and selenium (32 mcg/100 g). The high cholesterol content is the primary clinical consideration, particularly for individuals with cardiovascular disease or those on corticosteroids for rheumatoid arthritis. For type 2 diabetes, the negligible carbohydrate load makes quail eggs an excellent protein source with no direct glycaemic impact. Sodium is moderate at 141 mg/100 g. The eggs are classified as raw and unprocessed, are gluten-free, and are suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarian and halal diets.
common_user_queries: How much cholesterol is in quail eggs?
Are quail eggs good for high blood pressure?
How many quail eggs equal one chicken egg?
Are quail eggs safe for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01060014, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, and additional micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 01140 (Egg, quail, whole, fresh, raw), which is an exact food match. Both are approved primary sources.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; eggs naturally contain no dietary fibre. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central NDB 01140. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g — eggs naturally contain no vitamin C. Saturated fat (3.56 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (4.32 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.32 g/100 g), and micronutrient values not reported by HPB were sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01140 (Egg, quail, whole, fresh, raw), which is an exact food match. Quail egg; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.41 g/100 g); glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel for 'not applicable' and gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. The cholesterol value of 844 mg/100 g is exceptionally high and clinically significant — consistent across both HPB and USDA sources for quail eggs, reflecting the proportionally large yolk mass relative to total egg weight. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained: all fields set to numeric values are supported by HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central NDB 01140.
